quickStart.title: Get productive in 30 seconds
steps: Open Windsurf and press Cmd/Ctrl+L to open Cascade.
Pick Chat mode for questions or Write mode when you want code changes.
Choose a model in the selector below the Cascade input; start with a recommended low-cost model, then escalate for harder tasks.
Use Tab suggestions for inline flow, and escalate to Cascade only when the task needs broader context or tool use.
Create a workspace rule or workflow in .windsurf/ if you find yourself repeating the same instructions.

dailyWorkflows.title: Inline coding with Tab first
dailyWorkflows.description: Use Windsurf Tab for small edits, import insertion, and cursor jumps before involving the full agent.
dailyWorkflows.command: Accept with Tab, cancel with Esc, or accept incrementally word-by-word.

dailyWorkflows.title: Escalate to Cascade Write mode for real tasks
dailyWorkflows.description: When the change spans multiple files or needs tool calls, switch to Write mode and describe the task directly.
dailyWorkflows.command: Cmd/Ctrl+L → Write mode → Implement this feature and update tests.

dailyWorkflows.title: Use context-aware continuation
dailyWorkflows.description: Make a manual edit, then ask Cascade to continue from your last action instead of re-explaining everything.
dailyWorkflows.command: After editing manually, prompt: continue my work and finish wiring this up.

dailyWorkflows.title: Capture repetitive playbooks as workflows
dailyWorkflows.description: Save a deployment, PR triage, or test-fix routine as a markdown workflow and invoke it by slash command.

powerMoves.title: Exploit real-time collaboration awareness
powerMoves.description: Let Cascade observe your terminal activity, chat history, and recent editor actions, then ask it to pick up where you left off.
powerMoves.command: Make a few manual changes, then tell Cascade: continue my work, keep the current direction, and finish the edge cases.
powerMoves.why: This is one of Windsurf's distinct strengths and reduces context restating.

powerMoves.title: Use rules + memories + workflows together
powerMoves.description: Keep durable coding standards in rules, let Cascade store useful workspace memories, and turn multi-step procedures into workflows.
powerMoves.command: Store coding conventions in .windsurf/rules, ask Cascade to create a memory for repo-specific facts, and save repeated procedures in .windsurf/workflows/.
powerMoves.why: You get persistent context without bloating every prompt.

powerMoves.title: Tune auto-execution conservatively
powerMoves.description: Use Off, Auto, or Turbo based on task sensitivity and your trust in the model and environment.
powerMoves.command: Keep Off for sensitive repos, Auto for normal development, and Turbo only when the command deny list is already hardened.
powerMoves.why: Windsurf becomes fast without silently turning into a risky shell bot.

gotchas.problem: Cascade can stop after reaching its tool-call budget for a prompt.
gotchas.solution: If the trajectory stalls, type continue to resume from where it left off; each continue counts as a new prompt.

gotchas.problem: Turbo mode auto-executes commands and browser controls except items on your deny list.
gotchas.solution: Use Turbo only in well-understood repos with a strict deny list; otherwise stay on Off or Auto.
Use revert carefully, especially after larger changes; checkpoint mentally before clicking it.

gotchas.problem: If ignored files are not configured, Cascade may consider files you do not want it touching.
gotchas.solution: Maintain .codeiumignore at the workspace root and, for enterprise setups, use a global ~/.codeium/.codeiumignore if needed.

gotchas.problem: Too many rules or overly verbose workflows can make behavior inconsistent.
gotchas.solution: Keep rules concise and specific, and keep each workflow focused on a single repeatable process.

slashCommands.description: Run a saved Cascade workflow stored as markdown in .windsurf/workflows/.
slashCommands.tip: This is the main slash-command pattern Windsurf documents officially for repeatable task automation.

slashCommands.description: Example workflow pattern for processing PR feedback one comment at a time.
Example workflow pattern for standardized commits and PR creation.
slashCommands.tip: Turn your team's git checklist into a reusable command.

slashCommands.description: Example workflow pattern for deployment steps and verification.
slashCommands.tip: Best when your deploy sequence is stable and CLI-driven.

slashCommands.description: Example workflow pattern for on-demand vulnerability or security review steps.
slashCommands.tip: Useful for release gates or pre-merge checks.

config.description: Per-conversation model selection for Cascade across Windsurf and provider-hosted models, including BYOK options.
config.recommended: Start with a recommended cheaper model and switch up only for harder reasoning or larger context windows.

config.description: Controls command/browser auto-execution behavior in Cascade: Off, Auto, or Turbo.
config.recommended: Use Off for sensitive work, Auto for common dev tasks, Turbo only with strong guardrails.

config.description: Gitignore-style file for excluding paths from Windsurf viewing, editing, creating, and indexing behavior.
config.recommended: Add generated files, secrets, vendor dirs, and anything you do not want the agent touching.

config.description: Workspace rules directory for persistent coding instructions tied to the project.
config.recommended: Store concise repo conventions here instead of repeating them in every prompt.

config.description: Global rules file applied across workspaces.
config.recommended: Use for personal coding habits; keep project-specific rules in the workspace.

config.description: Markdown workflow directory discovered from the workspace, subdirectories, and parent directories up to git root.
config.recommended: Create one workflow per repeatable procedure and invoke it as a slash command.

config.description: Windsurf Tab can run in Autocomplete or Supercomplete mode, with optional clipboard context and Tab-to-Import/Tab-to-Jump toggles.
config.recommended: Use Supercomplete for richer edits; enable clipboard context only if the extra context is worth the privacy tradeoff.

proTips: Windsurf shines when you mix manual edits with agent follow-through instead of treating it as a one-shot black box.
Use Tab for tiny deltas, Cascade for multi-file work, and workflows for repeatable operational playbooks.
Rules should encode stable standards; workflows should encode ordered procedures.
If Cascade feels noisy, reduce prompt scope and clean up rules before changing models.
